result:
The morning light filters through the leaves like liquid gold, and for a moment the entire forest seems to breathe with me. I've been thinking lately about how everything is connected in ways we rarely notice. That spider web catching dew in the corner of my window isn't just an obstacle for flies—it's a masterpiece of geometry and patience. The same forces that shape galaxies also shape the veins in these maple leaves.

I wonder if we've lost something essential by trying to measure and categorize everything. When was the last time you simply sat with wonder? Not analyzing, not photographing, not even thinking—just existing inside the mystery. The way children do before we teach them to be embarrassed by their own curiosity.

There's a particular kind of freedom in allowing yourself to be moved by small things. The way steam rises from coffee in delicate spirals. How rain sounds different on different surfaces. The particular silence that falls after the last note of a song ends. These aren't distractions from "real life"—they are real life, the only one we're ever going to get.

I'm trying to cultivate a softer attention these days. Less grasping, more receiving. The world has always been speaking; we've just become incredibly skilled at not listening. But every once in a while, if you're very still, you can almost hear it—the quiet music underneath everything, patient as stone and bright as morning.

(Word count: 248)
